summary:
Frederick II (1194–1250 AD), Holy Roman Emperor and King of Sicily, was the most intellectually brilliant and controversial ruler of medieval Europe — called by his contemporaries both 'Stupor Mundi' (Wonder of the World) and the Antichrist — a ruler who defied papal authority repeatedly, communicated with Muslim philosophers, conducted a crusade that recovered Jerusalem through diplomacy rather than warfare, and presided over a court in Sicily that continued the [[event:3041|Norman Kingdom's]] tradition of multicultural intellectual synthesis. Born in Sicily to a German Hohenstaufen father and Norman-Sicilian mother, Frederick grew up in Palermo speaking Arabic, Greek, Latin, French, German, and Italian.

Frederick II's career was a sustained confrontation with papal authority. He was excommunicated four times yet remained the most powerful secular ruler in Europe; he led the Sixth Crusade while excommunicated and in 1229 negotiated a ten-year treaty with Sultan al-Kamil of Egypt that returned Jerusalem, Bethlehem, Nazareth, and a corridor to the sea to Christian control — an extraordinary diplomatic achievement accomplished without a battle. The papacy, which had urged crusade by force, was furious. Frederick's court at Palermo and Foggia patronized Arabic, Latin, and Greek scholarship — he corresponded with Islamic philosophers, conducted zoological experiments described in his treatise 'De Arte Venandi cum Avibus' (On the Art of Hunting with Birds), and was among the first Europeans to patronize the translation of Aristotle from Arabic. His Code of Melfi (1231) created the most rational legal code in medieval Europe. Frederick personified the tension between medieval universalist claims of Church and Empire, and his conflicts with the papacy prefigured the Reformation's challenge to papal authority three centuries later.
